summary.en: Osaka's hub for youth culture, street fashion, and vintage trends, America-mura is a vibrant, quirky district packed with independent boutiques, cafes, and eclectic street art.
An up-and-coming foodie paradise, Fukushima buzzes with hidden izakayas, innovative restaurants, and lively standing bars, popular with locals seeking quality and a modern Osaka vibe.
Discover a tranquil, moss-covered temple alleyway leading to a maze of charming izakayas and traditional eateries, a hidden gem amidst the Namba bustle.
A chic and trendy district known for its stylish independent boutiques, designer furniture stores, specialty coffee shops, and a sophisticated, relaxed atmosphere.
A charming bohemian district brimming with renovated traditional houses, independent cafes, vintage shops, and vibrant street art, offering a nostalgic and artistic escape from the city bustle.
Step back in time to 'New World,' an iconic, retro district defined by Tsutenkaku Tower, neon lights, cheap eats, and a nostalgic, working-class charm.
A lively, authentic Osaka neighborhood renowned for its endless array of izakayas, local eateries, and the bustling Tenjinbashisuji Shotengai, offering a true taste of local life.

description.en: A hidden gem cocktail bar renowned for its skilled mixologists, extensive spirit selection, and sophisticated, dimly lit ambiance, often requiring a keen eye to find.
A vibrant and quirky ice cream shop offering unique flavors and Instagrammable treats, embodying America-mura's youthful and creative spirit.
A unique and popular hot dog stand, offering creative toppings and delicious gourmet hot dogs, a perfect representation of America-mura's street food scene.
A large shopping complex in the heart of America-mura, housing independent boutiques, cafes, a cinema, and sometimes hosting local events, serving as a hub for youth culture.
A large and iconic mural symbolizing peace and unity, serving as a prominent piece of street art and a popular photo spot in America-mura.
An independent vintage clothing store renowned for its curated selection of unique American and European vintage fashion, reflecting America-mura's distinctive style.
A small, independent ramen shop serving deeply flavorful broths and expertly prepared noodles, a beloved spot for a hearty meal in the area.
Known as Mitsu Park, but affectionately called Triangle Park, this central plaza is a popular gathering spot for youth, street performers, and people-watching.
A sophisticated, hidden cocktail bar known for its expert mixologists, intimate atmosphere, and creative drink menu.
Explore the maze of narrow alleyways densely packed with an eclectic mix of independent restaurants, cafes, and bars, a true paradise for food lovers.
Wander through various alleyways to discover vibrant murals, graffiti, and creative street art pieces, reflecting Fukushima's modern and artistic edge.
A local Shinto shrine, smaller and more intimate than its larger counterparts, offering a peaceful retreat and insight into community traditions.
A specialized soba (buckwheat noodle) restaurant, praised for its handmade noodles and delicate, flavorful broths, a local favorite for traditional Japanese cuisine.
A vibrant and popular standing bar, offering a wide selection of drinks and delicious small bites, ideal for experiencing local nightlife and conversation.
A popular local standing bar, known for its lively atmosphere, affordable drinks, and simple yet delicious izakaya fare, a genuine Fukushima experience.
A highly-regarded yakitori (grilled chicken skewers) restaurant, famous for its expertly prepared skewers and cozy, authentic izakaya ambiance.
A stylish concept store featuring curated selections of local and independent designer goods, fashion, and lifestyle items, embodying Horie's chic aesthetic.
A discreet independent art gallery showcasing contemporary Japanese art, often featuring emerging local talents.
A tranquil urban park, providing a green escape amidst the trendy streets, popular for relaxing, picnics, and people-watching.
A small, dedicated coffee roaster and cafe, celebrated for its expertly roasted beans and artisanal approach to coffee, a must-visit for serious enthusiasts.
A chic specialty coffee shop known for its meticulous pour-over techniques, minimalist design, and high-quality beans, appealing to coffee connoisseurs.
The main artery of Minami-Horie, famous for its concentration of fashion boutiques, interior design stores, specialty cafes, and stylish showrooms.
A charming and authentic Italian restaurant, offering a refined dining experience with homemade pasta and seasonal ingredients in a stylish setting.

name.zh-Hans: Ristorante La Cialda (拉恰尔达餐厅)
name.zh-Hant: Ristorante La Cialda (拉恰爾達餐廳)

description.en: A highly-rated, independent ramen shop serving rich, flavorful broths and perfectly cooked noodles, a local favorite for its consistent quality.
A cozy, vintage-themed cafe known for its unique interior decor, homemade sweets, and a relaxed ambiance perfect for people-watching.
A narrow, old-school shopping arcade that retains a nostalgic charm, lined with local businesses, quirky shops, and traditional facades.
Explore the vibrant, ever-changing murals, quirky installations, and hidden artistic expressions adorning the walls and alleys throughout the neighborhood.
A delightful little Italian restaurant offering homemade pasta and classic dishes, popular among locals for its warm atmosphere and delicious food.
A popular vintage clothing and antique shop, embodying Nakazakicho's unique fashion sense and commitment to sustainability and individuality.
An iconic cafe housed in a beautifully preserved traditional Japanese house, offering a serene atmosphere, delicious coffee, and light meals. A true Nakazakicho institution.

name.zh-Hans: Salon de AManTo (沙龙 de AManTo)
name.zh-Hant: Salon de AManTo (沙龍 de AManTo)

description.en: A small, independent art gallery that frequently showcases works by local Osaka artists, reflecting the bohemian spirit of Nakazakicho.
A small, charming local spot serving authentic Osaka-style okonomiyaki (savory pancake) in a friendly, no-frills setting.
A classic, old-school coffee shop and bar in Shinsekai, offering a glimpse into Osaka's past with its retro decor and local patrons.

name.zh-Hans: Bar & Café Den-en (田园酒吧咖啡馆)
name.zh-Hant: Bar & Café Den-en (田園酒吧咖啡館)

description.en: The 'God of Things as They Ought to Be,' Billiken is Shinsekai's lucky charm. Rub his feet for good fortune! Multiple statues are found throughout the area.
A bustling street food stand offering delicious dote-yaki (beef sinew stew) and freshly made takoyaki (octopus balls), quintessential Shinsekai street food.
A narrow, lively alley filled with small, traditional eateries, shogi (Japanese chess) parlors, and pachinko halls, offering an authentic glimpse into old Osaka life.
A small, beloved oden (Japanese hot pot stew) shop, serving comforting and flavorful dishes, a true local gem hidden in the alleys of Shinsekai.
Step into a small, old-fashioned arcade filled with vintage video games and classic pachinko machines, evoking a strong sense of Showa-era nostalgia.
A quintessential Shinsekai standing bar, perfect for a quick drink and cheap, delicious snacks while soaking in the local atmosphere.
The iconic symbol of Shinsekai, this observation tower offers panoramic views of Osaka and embodies the retro charm of the neighborhood.
A cozy, unassuming local bar, often tucked away in a small alley, offering a friendly atmosphere and a chance to mingle with Osaka's regulars.
A legendary, no-frills sushi restaurant renowned for its incredibly fresh seafood, generous portions, and affordable prices. Expect a queue!
A traditional shop specializing in koji (fermented rice) products, miso, and other fermented foods, showcasing an important aspect of Japanese culinary heritage.
A significant Shinto shrine dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, the deity of scholarship, and host to the Tenjin Matsuri, one of Japan's top festivals.
Japan's longest shopping street, stretching over 2.6 km, filled with local shops, eateries, pharmacies, and a vibrant, everyday atmosphere.
A small, dedicated gyoza (dumpling) specialist, serving crispy, juicy dumplings with various fillings, a perfect snack or light meal.
A bustling local market where fresh produce, seafood, and everyday goods are sold, offering a sensory experience and a true slice of Osaka's culinary heart.
A bustling and popular standing bar (tachinomi) known for its extensive selection of sake, craft beers, and delicious small plates, a perfect local experience.
A truly unique architectural oddity where the Hanshin Expressway passes directly through the 5th, 6th, and 7th floors of the office building.
Perhaps the world's cheapest vending machines, offering a surprise drink for just a 10-yen coin. A famous one is located in the Fukushima area.
Home to Gakutensoku, Japan's first automaton, which was lost and later revived. The museum offers a range of interactive science exhibits.
An iconic 173-meter-tall skyscraper with a unique structure, featuring a floating garden observatory that offers panoramic views of Osaka.
Located at Hozenji Temple, this moss-covered statue of a Buddhist deity is splashed with water by worshippers for good fortune.
A quirky bar filled with a massive collection of unlicensed, knockoff Star Wars merchandise from around the globe.
A former brothel turned restaurant, this building features incredibly elaborate and extravagant architecture from the Taisho era.
Towering 300 meters over the city, this skyscraper features an observation deck, art museum, hotel, and Japan's largest department store.
A legendary standing-only street food stall famous for its blowtorched tuna, fresh uni, and charismatic, chain-smoking owner.
One of Japan's most famous landmarks, this historic castle played a major role in the unification of Japan during the sixteenth century.

prompt.en: Fukushima is a business hub. Find a restaurant advertising its lunch special and snap a photo of the delicious-looking deal.
Many restaurants and bars are tucked under the elevated train tracks. Capture the unique atmosphere of one of these spots.
Find a view that captures both a modern glass skyscraper and a small, traditional Japanese building in the same frame.
Take an atmospheric photo of the warm light and inviting noren curtain at the entrance of an izakaya.
Seek out a small, local shrine tucked between larger buildings and capture its quiet presence.
Find a bridge or spot along the river and take a scenic photo of the water with the city buildings in the background.
Explore the backstreets to find a piece of unexpected street art or graffiti. Snap a picture of your discovery.
Capture the energy of the neighborhood with a dynamic shot of a train passing on the elevated tracks.
Find a row of colorful vending machines and take a vibrant photo.
Find a piece of modern public art or sculpture and take a creative selfie with it.
Wander down a narrow alley packed with restaurants and capture the dense, delicious atmosphere.
Use the glass facade of a modern building to capture an interesting reflection of the street or sky.
Find a row of neatly parked bicycles, a common sight in residential and commercial areas, and take a photo emphasizing the pattern.
Spot a stylish, independent coffee shop and take a picture of its inviting storefront.
Look down! Find one of Osaka's decorative manhole covers and take a photo of its unique design.
Capture the lively, casual atmosphere of a 'tachinomi' (standing bar) from the outside.
Find a small park or a building covered in ivy and capture this patch of nature in the urban landscape.
As evening falls, take a photo of the glowing signs and streetlights that bring the neighborhood to life.
Find a classic red paper lantern ('chochin') and take an atmospheric photo of it lighting up the stone-paved alley.
Inspired by the Fudo Myo-o statue, find some interesting moss or plant life growing in an unexpected urban place.
Capture the lively spirit of 'Back Namba'. Look for a bustling tachinomi bar, a unique sign, or a group of friends enjoying the night.
Take a selfie on the iconic, wet stone-paved ground of Hozenji Yokocho.
Get a mouth-watering close-up shot of a local specialty like okonomiyaki or takoyaki being prepared at a street-side stall.
Find a restaurant with a beautiful or interesting 'noren' (fabric divider) at its entrance and take a picture.
Use a low angle to capture a dramatic perspective shot looking down one of the narrow, atmospheric alleyways.
This area is full of tiny, hidden bars. Find one with a mysterious or quirky entrance and snap a photo.
Capture the steam rising from a food stall or the smoke from a grill, adding to the alley's atmosphere.
Frame a shot that shows the traditional alleyways with a modern Namba skyscraper visible in the background.
Find a beautifully handwritten menu outside a restaurant and take a photo of the calligraphy.
At night, use the light from the lanterns to create an interesting photo with long shadows.
Take a photo of your drink at a local bar, with the unique atmosphere of the place in the background.
In a narrow alley, point your camera straight up to capture the slice of sky framed by the old buildings.
Find a funny, strange, or beautifully designed sign for a shop or restaurant and capture it.
Take a close-up photo of the texture of an old wooden wall, a stone path, or a weathered sign.
After a splash of water (or rain), find a puddle on the stone path and capture the reflection of the lanterns.
Find a small stool or bench outside a shop and take a photo from its perspective.
Find a sign or building that looks like it's straight out of a retro sci-fi movie and capture its essence.
The god of 'things as they ought to be' is everywhere here. Find a Billiken statue and pose with it for good luck.
Frame a shot of a colorful street with the Tsutenkaku Tower looming in the background.
This area is famous for its shogi (Japanese chess) clubs. Without being intrusive, capture the atmosphere of intense concentration from outside.
Take a picture of a giant, colorful kushikatsu restaurant sign. Bonus points if it has a 'no double-dipping' warning!
Capture the bright, chaotic, and colorful entrance of a pachinko parlor.
Take a photo looking down the narrow, covered Jan Jan Yokocho alley, capturing its old-school vibe.
Find one of the giant 3D signs (like a fugu lantern or a chef) and take a funny selfie with it.
Find one of the old movie theaters in the area and take a photo of its vintage posters or facade.
Capture the nostalgic glow of a retro arcade or a classic claw machine.
Look up and take a photo that captures the chaotic, overlapping layers of neon and printed signs.
Shinsekai has a unique style. Capture a detail of local fashion, like a cool jacket or interesting shoes (be respectful!).
The pufferfish is a symbol of Shinsekai. Find a giant fugu lantern and get a great shot of it.
Find a shop or restaurant that looks like it hasn't changed since the Showa era (1926-1989) and capture its nostalgic feel.
Find a takoyaki stand and take an action shot of the chef expertly flipping the octopus balls.
After sunset, capture the reflection of the bright neon signs in a puddle or on a wet street.
Stand near the base of the Tsutenkaku Tower and take a photo looking straight up for a dizzying perspective.
Take a portrait of an interesting object that you feel represents the unique character of Shinsekai.
Capture the sense of scale of the Tenjinbashisuji Shotengai. Try a low-angle shot looking down the seemingly endless covered street.
Find a stall in the Tenma Market selling something unusual or colorful—like fresh seafood, pickles, or sweets—and take an artistic photo.
Find a quiet corner near the Osaka Tenmangu Shrine or a small local park and capture a moment of tranquility amidst the bustle.
Tenma is great for sake. Find a bar or shop with an impressive display of sake bottles and snap a photo.
Bicycles are a common sight in the shotengai. Get a cool action shot of a local cycling through the arcade.
Many sushi restaurants have plastic food models in their windows. Take a photo of a particularly realistic or extravagant display.
Look up! The Tenjinbashisuji arcade has unique decorations hanging from the ceiling. Capture a photo of them.
Visit the grounds of Osaka Tenmangu and take a close-up photo of an interesting detail, like a stone lantern, a wooden ema plaque, or a carving.
Find a lively 'tachinomi' (standing bar) and capture its energetic vibe from the street.
Search for a traditional shop selling something like tea, knives, or kimono, and photograph its classic storefront.
Find a gyoza shop and try to capture a photo of the dumplings sizzling on the grill.
Take a photo from one of the bridges crossing the Ogawa River, capturing the view of the water and the city.
At Tenmangu Shrine, find the wooden 'ema' plaques where students write wishes for exams. Photograph the collection of hopeful messages.
Many shops in the shotengai have their own cute or funny mascots. Find one and take its portrait.
Capture a photo of a colorful, hand-written sign advertising a sale or special offer in the shopping arcade.
Explore the side streets off the main arcade to find a tiny, charming restaurant and capture its hidden appeal.
Find a spot where you can see Osaka Castle peeking out from between modern buildings and snap a photo of the contrast.
Grab a coffee at a local cafe and take a photo from your seat, capturing the flow of people in the arcade.
